full_response:
We work in Schwarzschild coordinates (t , r , θ , φ) with the line‐element  

ds² = −(1−2GM/r) dt² + (1−2GM/r)⁻¹ dr² + r²(dθ² + sin²θ dφ²).        (1)

1. World-line of a “static” clock  
“Static” means the clock remains at fixed spatial Schwarzschild coordinates:

dr = dθ = dφ = 0.                                                 (2)

Insert (2) into (1):

ds² = −(1−2GM/r) dt².                                            (3)

2. Proper time of the clock  
For timelike world-lines, the proper time interval dτ is related to the line element by

ds² = −c² dτ².                                                   (4)

(We will set c = 1 until the very last step, if desired.)

Using (3) in (4):

−(1−2GM/r) dt² = − dτ²   
⇒ dτ = √(1−2GM/r) dt.                                           (5)

3. Clock at spatial infinity  
At r → ∞, the Schwarzschild metric approaches the Minkowski metric, so for a static observer there dr = dθ = dφ = 0 as well, but g_tt → −1.  
Hence

dτ_∞ = dt.                                                       (6)

4. Tick rate comparison  
The rate at which the clock at radius r elapses proper time, relative to the clock at infinity, is

(dτ/dt)_r  /  (dτ/dt)_∞ = √(1−2GM/r).                          (7)

Equivalently,

dτ_r = √(1−2GM/r) dτ_∞.                                         (8)

Thus the static clock at radius r ticks slower by the redshift factor √(1−2GM/r).

Restoring c,

dτ_r = √(1−2GM/(rc²)) dτ_∞.                                     (9)

\boxed{\displaystyle \frac{\text{rate of clock at } r}{\text{rate at infinity}} = \sqrt{1-\frac{2GM}{r\,c^{2}}}}  

(The proper time interval at radius r is smaller by this factor; i.e. the clock deeper in the gravitational field runs slower.)

Confidence: 0.83
